original_hypothesis.title: Changing Marketplace Settings Contract Desynchronizes Vesting Manager
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: If the admin updates the marketplaceSetting address to a new contract that points to a different vestingManager, existing active listings will break because spotPurchase will attempt to call completePurchase on the new manager.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The marketplace relies on the settings contract to locate the vesting manager. If settings are upgraded without migrating the escrowed vestings, the new manager won't have the seller's tokens, causing purchases to revert and trapping listed assets.
preconditions: Admin calls setMarketplaceSettingAddress to point to a new config
New config has a different vestingManager address
Active listings exist in the old vestingManager

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Dynamic resolution of vestingManager during spotPurchase
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Purchases revert because new manager lacks the escrowed tokens
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Operational failure, potential loss of funds if old manager cannot be accessed

economic_questions: Is there a migration path for escrowed vestings?
Can sellers still unlist from the old manager?
validation_requirements: Simulate settings upgrade with active listings
Verify if unlistVesting also breaks (it does, as it uses dynamic resolution)

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Traps user funds in deprecated contracts

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Inability to sell or recover tokens

validation_result.mechanism: dynamic resolution desynchronization on admin config change
validation_result.impact_summary: Changing marketplaceSetting (or its vestingManager pointer) while active listings exist causes all spotPurchase and unlistVesting calls to revert, trapping sellers' escrowed vesting tokens in the old VestingManager with no marketplace-level recovery path.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The SecondSwap_Marketplace contract resolves the vestingManager address dynamically at call time via IMarketplaceSetting(marketplaceSetting).vestingManager(). When a listing is created, the seller's vesting tokens are transferred to the old VestingManager's address via transferVesting(seller, address(this), amount). If the admin then calls setMarketplaceSettingAddress() with a new settings contract pointing to a different VestingManager, all subsequent spotPurchase and unlistVesting calls resolve to the NEW VestingManager. The new manager does not hold the escrowed tokens, so completePurchase (which calls transferVesting(address(this), buyer, amount) where address(this) is the new manager) will revert with 'insufficient balance'. Similarly, unlistVesting will also fail because the new manager cannot return tokens it doesn't hold. There is no migration mechanism, no check for active listings, and no way through the marketplace to access the old manager's escrowed tokens after the change.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is confirmed by three pieces of code evidence: (1) setMarketplaceSettingAddress at SecondSwap_Marketplace.sol performs a bare assignment with no state migration, (2) spotPurchase and unlistVesting dynamically resolve vestingManager at call time rather than using the manager stored at listing creation, and (3) VestingManager.completePurchase/unlistVesting call transferVesting(address(this), ...) where address(this) must hold escrowed tokens. The same issue exists via SecondSwap_MarketplaceSetting.setManager() which changes the vestingManager pointer within the same settings contract.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No validation that active listings exist, no migration of escrowed tokens, no check that the new settings contract points to the same vestingManager. A simple assignment breaks all active listings.
Dynamically resolves vestingManager at call time. If marketplaceSetting changed since listing creation, this calls completePurchase on a different manager that doesn't hold the escrowed tokens.
Same dynamic resolution issue. Sellers cannot recover their escrowed tokens because unlistVesting calls the new manager, which doesn't hold the tokens.
Calls transferVesting(address(this), buyer, amount). If 'this' is the new VestingManager, it has zero vesting balance, so the call reverts with 'insufficient balance' in StepVesting.transferVesting.
Same desynchronization can be triggered by changing vestingManager within the existing settings contract, without changing the settings contract itself.

calling_sequence: 1. Admin calls setMarketplaceSettingAddress(_newSettings) - passes auth check, updates marketplaceSetting to new address
2. New settings contract has vestingManager pointing to a different VestingManager instance (ManagerB)
3. Buyer calls spotPurchase() for an existing listing that was escrowed in the old VestingManager (ManagerA)
5. _handleTransfers calls ManagerB.getVestingTokenAddress() - this works since it just queries the vesting plan
6. Token transfers complete (buyer pays currency to seller/feeCollector)
10. ManagerB has zero vesting balance => REVERT with 'insufficient balance'
11. Entire spotPurchase transaction reverts, but buyer already approved tokens and listing appears valid
12. Similarly, seller calling unlistVesting also reverts because ManagerB.unlistVesting calls transferVesting(address(ManagerB), seller, amount) with zero balance
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The defect is in the dynamic resolution pattern combined with the lack of migration. At listing time, tokens are escrowed in VestingManager_A (via listVesting -> transferVesting(seller, address(VestingManager_A), amount)). After setMarketplaceSettingAddress changes the settings, all marketplace operations resolve to VestingManager_B. VestingManager_B.completePurchase calls transferVesting(address(VestingManager_B), buyer, amount) but VestingManager_B._vestings has no balance for the vesting plan. The transaction reverts at SecondSwap_StepVesting.sol transferVesting: require(grantorVesting.totalAmount - grantorVesting.amountClaimed >= _amount). Sellers cannot unlist either, as unlistVesting has the identical dynamic resolution pattern.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment scripts show the marketplace, settings, and vesting manager are wired together during initial deployment (DeployMarketplace.ts wires vestingManager.setMarketplace, DeployMarketplaceSetting.ts passes vestingManager to constructor). However, no scripts address migration scenarios. The setMarketplaceSettingAddress and setManager functions are available post-deployment without any migration safeguards.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: This is not a traditional attack but an admin operational hazard. Scenario: (1) Multiple sellers have active listings with tokens escrowed in VestingManager_A. (2) Admin deploys new MarketplaceSetting contract pointing to VestingManager_B for a legitimate upgrade. (3) Admin calls setMarketplaceSettingAddress with the new settings contract. (4) All active listings become permanently broken - buyers can't purchase, sellers can't unlist. (5) The only recovery path is for the admin to revert the change or manually coordinate with the old VestingManager. The same scenario can occur via MarketplaceSetting.setManager() without changing the settings contract itself.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM-HIGH. All actively listed vesting tokens become inaccessible through the marketplace. Sellers cannot recover their escrowed tokens via unlistVesting. The tokens are not permanently lost (they still exist in the old VestingManager), but there is no marketplace-level recovery mechanism. The admin would need to either revert the change or set up the old VestingManager's marketplace pointer to a rescue contract. During the desynchronization period, all marketplace trading is effectively halted for existing listings.
recommended_mitigations: Store the vestingManager address at listing creation time in the Listing struct, and use the stored address for spotPurchase and unlistVesting operations instead of dynamically resolving it
Add a check in setMarketplaceSettingAddress and MarketplaceSetting.setManager that verifies no active listings exist (or requires a migration step)
Implement a migration function that can transfer escrowed vestings from the old VestingManager to the new one before switching
Add a two-phase commit for settings changes: announce the change, allow a grace period for sellers to unlist, then finalize
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy full system: MarketplaceSetting, VestingManager_A, Marketplace, StepVesting. 2. Create a vesting for seller, have seller list it (tokens escrowed in VestingManager_A). 3. Deploy VestingManager_B, deploy new MarketplaceSetting pointing to VestingManager_B. 4. Set VestingManager_B.marketplace = Marketplace address. 5. Call Marketplace.setMarketplaceSettingAddress(newSettings). 6. Attempt spotPurchase on existing listing - expect revert. 7. Attempt unlistVesting on existing listing - expect revert. 8. Verify tokens are stuck in VestingManager_A.
uncertainties: Whether the admin would realistically change the settings to point to a different VestingManager (vs. upgrading the VestingManager proxy in place)
Whether there are off-chain operational procedures that prevent this scenario
additional_testing_needed: Test the same desynchronization via MarketplaceSetting.setManager() changing vestingManager within the same settings contract
Verify that the old VestingManager's tokens can be recovered by setting up a rescue marketplace or directly updating the old manager's marketplace address
